subject: Work around docker rate limit in CSITs
commit_message:
Work around docker rate limit in CSITs

CSITs are failing regularly due to download rate limits that were
recently introduced on dockerhub.  Modified the run-csit script to make
nexus3 a mirror to be used instead of dockerhub.
This commit is primarily a PoC.

Issue-ID: POLICY-2895
Change-Id: I8687e4c5262be87b0f7007e4d0320260938e0c71
Signed-off-by: Jim Hahn <jrh3@att.com>

Patch Set 2:

This change is ready for review.

Patch Set 2:

(2 comments)

Unless we get a login that allows the jenkins jobs to avoid the docker download rate limit, I see the following options:
1) stop running CSITs every day
2) eliminate and/or combine CSITs - not sure if this would solve the problem or not
3) use a different docker server (e.g., nexus3)

Have any other ideas?  At this point, if we can't do this in run-csit.sh, then policy will probably arrange for it to be done in the policy setup.sh scripts instead.

Patch Set 2: -Code-Review

> Patch Set 2:
> 
> (2 comments)
> 
> Unless we get a login that allows the jenkins jobs to avoid the docker download rate limit, I see the following options:
> 1) stop running CSITs every day
> 2) eliminate and/or combine CSITs - not sure if this would solve the problem or not
> 3) use a different docker server (e.g., nexus3)
> 
> Have any other ideas?  At this point, if we can't do this in run-csit.sh, then policy will probably arrange for it to be done in the policy setup.sh scripts instead.

I think this run-csit.sh hack *can* be merged as a quick *temporary* solution despite my objections if it actually does help. I echo Bartek's question about *how* this helps though as I don't really understand how dockerhub is involved in ONAP policy images by default and how our ONAP Nexus would help for 3rd party images? (i.e. how does all this actually work *now* and how this change would alter it?)

For generally alleviating the pressure and avoid existing daily limit I'd propose disabling daily runs for a while (I think we're doing too much of that anyway). Trying to reduce the load by combining CSITs doesn't sound like a cost-effective solution though.

In the long run our view is that this should be solved by LF (provide a proper mirror for dockerhub if that kind of action really solves the problem and apply it automatically in Jenkins VMs etc) - is anyone aware of any ticket raised to them about this yet, or has it just been aired in mai and Rocket?

oops, don't mind the duplicate - I'm not used to comment replies being sent out immediately without pusing "reply" separately :P

Patch Set 2:

Yeah, I'm having the same issue with replies, but want to make sure it isn't merged, at least for the moment.

Honestly, I don't know that this will actually fix the problem.  For that matter, it might even break some of the component CSITs.  All I know is that our CSITs are failing due to the rate limit when trying to download sonatype/nexus, and this change appears to make it download from nexus3 (not even sure why it downloads sonatype/nexus).  As for the onap images, our docker compose yaml already specifies nexus3, so that shouldn't be rate limited anyway.  I'm wondering if it's just all of the jobs combined, not just CSITs, but the docker builds, etc., that use dockerhub for sonatype/nexus and alpine base images.  If that's the case, then this may fix the CSITs while leaving those other jobs broken.

Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

We could just make the change in the policy setup.sh scripts instead of run-csit.sh, to avoid impacting CSITs for other onap components.

Patch Set 2:

> Patch Set 2:
> 
> (1 comment)
> 
> We could just make the change in the policy setup.sh scripts instead of run-csit.sh, to avoid impacting CSITs for other onap components.

For PoC purposes that (or putting it to just *one* policy setup script) could be a better idea - it's more convincing to see it work in just one altered test while all the others are failing to the dockerhub limit at the same time.

The finer technical details of less intrusive docker daemon config change can be discussed if we find the general idea to work - at least a brute overwrite didn't seem to affect your current test.

Patch Set 2:

I'm going to abandon this for now.  Turns out that the latest rate limit failures in policy CSITs were when downloading mariadb.  We can easily address that via a change to policy's docker-compose.yml.  Then, only if we run into more rate issues (i.e., with sonatype/nexus), we'll update one or two of policy's setup.sh scripts to use this mirroring approach.

Thanks for all the feedback.

Abandoned

Not needed at this time
